skill.name: docker-workflow
skill.description: Guides Docker containerization workflows with multi-stage builds, Docker Compose setups, image optimization, debugging helpers, and production deployment practices.
skill.summary: Create, optimize, debug, and deploy Docker-based application workflows.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. AI review found no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection, but the helper script can expose container environment variables and alter local Docker resources. The Docker Compose examples also include hardcoded development credentials that require clear user caution before production use.

medium_findings.title: Helper Script Can Reveal Container Secrets
medium_findings.description: The helper script includes an env command that runs env inside a container and sorts the output. This is useful for debugging, but it can print tokens, passwords, and database URLs stored in container environment variables.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly executes docker exec on a selected container and prints all environment variables. This is legitimate Docker debugging behavior, but the sensitive data exposure risk is clear.

medium_findings.title: Cleanup Command Removes Local Docker Resources
medium_findings.description: The cleanup command force-prunes stopped containers, dangling images, unused volumes, and unused networks. This can remove local development data if users run it without understanding the scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prune commands are explicit and use -f. They target Docker resources rather than arbitrary host paths, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Examples Include Hardcoded Development Credentials
medium_findings.description: The Compose example contains default passwords, admin credentials, and a fallback SECRET_KEY. These are common in examples but unsafe if copied into production unchanged.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credentials and fallback secret are visible in Docker Compose configuration. Context shows they are sample values, which lowers severity but does not remove copy-paste production risk.

medium_findings.title: Helper Script Provides Interactive Container Access and Volume Backup
medium_findings.description: The script can open an interactive shell in a running container and archive mounted volumes to a user-selected destination. These actions are useful but can expose application files and mounted secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is intentional and scoped to Docker containers. It still gives broad access to container files and mounted data when run by a user with Docker permissions.

low_findings.title: Static Prompt-Injection Markers Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged role-token lines are service usernames and admin credentials in Docker Compose examples. No evidence found of text instructing the AI to ignore prior instructions or change the audit result.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious words are configuration keys and values, not natural-language instructions to the model. Manual context review did not reveal prompt injection content.

low_findings.title: Recursive Delete Findings Are Docker Cache Cleanup Examples
low_findings.description: The rm -rf patterns remove package-manager cache directories inside Docker build instructions. No evidence found of recursive deletion targeting host root or home directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are standard Dockerfile cleanup patterns for apt lists. They are in examples and do not reference host paths such as / or /home.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Obfuscation Signals Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto and obfuscation hits appear to be keyword collisions in Docker guidance and shell syntax. No evidence found of encoded payloads, hidden downloaders, or weak cryptography used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal helper-script commands and documentation headings at the cited locations. The repository contains shell commands, but not confirmed obfuscated execution logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker CLI Execution from Shell Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The script dispatches user-selected subcommands that call docker exec, docker inspect, docker logs, docker run, and Docker prune operations. Users should review actions before running it against sensitive containers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command dispatcher directly maps user commands to Docker operations. The commands are expected for this skill but carry local system impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Insecure Defaults in Compose Template
dangerous_patterns.description: The example stack publishes several service ports and uses default credentials for database, queue, monitoring, and admin services. These values should be replaced before any production deployment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is explicit configuration. It is presented as an example stack, so it is risky guidance rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Docker setup often becomes inconsistent across development, testing, and deployment. This skill gives structured workflows, examples, and helper commands for building reliable container workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains multi-stage Dockerfile patterns for Node.js, Python, Go, Java, and Rust.
Provides Docker Compose examples for web apps, databases, caches, queues, monitoring, and admin tools.
Documents build, run, logging, shell, cleanup, network, and deployment commands.
Includes a shell helper for container health checks, logs, stats, inspection, backups, and image analysis.
Gives production guidance for non-root users, health checks, image scanning, restart policies, and image optimization.
limitations: It provides templates and guidance, not a complete application-specific deployment plan.
Example credentials must be replaced before production use.
The helper script requires local Docker permissions and can affect local Docker resources.
Users must adapt ports, networks, volumes, and health checks to their own application stack.

use_cases.title: Containerize a Web Application
use_cases.description: Create a multi-stage Dockerfile and Compose setup for a frontend, backend, database, and cache.

use_cases.title: Standardize Development Environments
use_cases.description: Use Compose overrides, bind mounts, and hot reload settings to align local developer workflows.

use_cases.title: Prepare Images for Deployment
use_cases.description: Review image size, non-root execution, health checks, dependency caching, and production Compose settings.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Dockerfile
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one application service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the docker-workflow skill to create a simple Dockerfile for my application. Ask for the runtime, package manager, start command, and exposed port before drafting it.

prompt_templates.title: Add Docker Compose
prompt_templates.scenario: Compose setup for multiple local services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the docker-workflow skill to design a Docker Compose file for my app, database, and cache. Include health checks, networks, volumes, and development overrides.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize an Existing Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve build speed, image size, and security posture.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the docker-workflow skill to review my Dockerfile for image size, layer caching, non-root execution, dependency installation, and production readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Production Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the docker-workflow skill to audit my Docker Compose and Dockerfile setup for production. Identify insecure defaults, secrets handling gaps, exposed ports, health checks, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: I need to containerize a Node.js API with PostgreSQL.
output: A multi-stage Dockerfile outline with dependency caching and a non-root runtime user.
A Compose service plan for the API and PostgreSQL with health checks and a named volume.
A short checklist for replacing development secrets before deployment.

output_examples.input: My Docker image is too large and rebuilds slowly.
output: A review of layer ordering, dependency installation, and build context size.
Recommended .dockerignore entries and multi-stage build changes.
Commands to inspect image history and compare size after changes.

output_examples.input: Prepare my Compose stack for production review.
output: A prioritized list of risky defaults, exposed ports, and missing health checks.
Guidance for secrets management, restart policies, and network isolation.
A deployment checklist covering pull, restart, logs, and rollback validation.

best_practices: Replace all example passwords, admin accounts, and fallback secrets before using templates outside local development.
Run the helper script only against containers and Docker hosts that you control.
Use pinned base images, non-root users, health checks, and vulnerability scanning for production images.
anti_patterns: Do not copy example Compose credentials into production environments.
Do not run Docker cleanup commands before checking whether unused volumes contain needed local data.
Do not publish databases, queues, dashboards, or admin tools to public networks without authentication and network controls.

faq.question: Does this skill run Docker automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides guidance and includes a helper script. Users choose whether to run the script or commands locally.

faq.question: Can I use the examples in production?
faq.answer: Use them as starting points only. Replace credentials, review exposed ports, isolate networks, and configure secrets management first.

faq.question: Which application stacks are covered?
faq.answer: The examples cover Node.js, Python, Go, Java, Rust, PostgreSQL, Redis, RabbitMQ, Nginx, Prometheus, Grafana, pgAdmin, and Mailhog.

faq.question: What does the helper script do?
faq.answer: It supports health checks, inspection, logs, stats, shell access, cleanup, image sizing, networking, ports, environment display, backups, and optimization review.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It includes executable Docker helper commands and templates with development credentials. These are useful but require user review before use.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
